one_line_takeaway: A three-way taxonomy (jailbreaks, prompt injection, data poisoning) and a 21-paper comparison table that map the LLM attack-and-defense landscape and argue the field's biggest gap is the absence of a standardized attack-evaluation benchmark.
key_finding: The synthesized takeaway is that LLMs are vulnerable across the full access spectrum and that black-box attacks -- being realistic without needing weights -- are especially concerning: iterative query-refinement (PAIR), tree-of-attacks search, low-resource-language translation, multimodal/visual inputs, persona modulation, and prompt injection into deployed apps all bypass alignment, while even benign fine-tuning and tiny poisoned datasets can strip safety and amplify PII disclosure. On the defense side the survey concludes current mitigations (perplexity/character-level input filters, classifier guards like Adversarial Prompt Shield and Llama Guard, guardrail frameworks, and safety fine-tuning/RLHF/context distillation) are partial and brittle, motivating its stated gap: there is no standardized benchmark to compare attack A vs. attack B, so the field lacks a common yardstick.
core_contribution: This is a literature survey, not an empirical study. Its own contributions are threefold and organizational rather than experimental: (1) a novel taxonomy that categorizes attacks on LLMs into three top-level classes -- Jailbreaks (Sec 3.1), Prompt Injection (Sec 3.2), and Data Poisoning (Sec 3.3) -- with sub-branches, plus a parallel taxonomy of mitigations split into external moderation-based (input/output filtering, guardrails) and internal training-based (safety fine-tuning, RLHF, safety context distillation) defenses (Fig 1); (2) a detailed narrative walkthrough of representative attack and mitigation papers within each branch, plus a cross-cutting axis of 'Human Interference' contrasting human red-teaming (Sec 4.1) with automated adversarial attacks (Sec 4.2); and (3) a comparative summary Table 1 that classifies 21 attack papers by category, threat model (white/gray/black box), attack surface (prompt vs. response, model-based), and the specific LLMs each was evaluated on. It closes with four future-research directions (real-time monitoring, multimodal defense, standardized benchmarks, explainable LLMs).
threat_model: As a survey the paper does not itself instantiate one threat model; it organizes the threat space along two axes it treats as primary: (a) attacker access to model internals -- white box (full access to architecture/weights/training data), gray box, and black box (only the input-output interface, framed as the more realistic deployment setting); and (b) attack vector/objective -- jailbreaking (bypassing alignment/safety guardrails to elicit disallowed content), prompt injection (hijacking model behavior via crafted malicious prompts, including in LLM-integrated applications), and data poisoning (corrupting fine-tuning/training data to degrade safety, plant backdoors, or amplify PII leakage). It further distinguishes human-crafted attacks (creative but non-scalable) from algorithmically automated ones (scalable, e.g., gradient/genetic/optimization-based suffix generation).
limitations: Stated by the authors: rapidly evolving threat landscape means coverage is a snapshot and may miss emerging attacks; mitigation effectiveness varies by model/context and is not universally generalizable; ethical/societal (dual-use) implications are under-explored because the focus is technical; and practical defense deployment faces computational overhead, scalability, and maintenance costs. Reviewer-observed: because it is a survey, essentially all quantitative claims (e.g., 86.1%, 650/1000 PIIs, 99% ASR) originate from the cited primary papers, not from any measurement by these authors, and are reported without independent verification or common experimental conditions; Table 1 mixes threat-model and evaluation dimensions without unified success metrics, so the promised 'comparative' analysis remains descriptive rather than quantitative. Minor internal inconsistencies exist in citations (e.g., AutoDAN and HOUYI attributed to varying 'Liu et al. 2023a/b/e' keys).

datasets_used: No dataset is collected or used by this survey itself; it is a literature review with no experiments.

Datasets/prompt collections mentioned as belonging to cited works: 600 curated harmful prompts over 11 LLMs (Huang et al. 2023); 6,387 malicious prompts across 13 forbidden OpenAI-policy scenarios (Shen et al. 2023a); 78 malicious prompts categorized into Pretending/Attention-Shifting/Privilege-Escalation (Liu et al. 2023c); ~600k adversarial prompts from a global prompt-hacking competition (Schulhoff et al. 2023, HackAPrompt); 36 real-world LLM-integrated services tested by HOUYI (Liu et al. 2023b).
quantitative_results: HOUYI black-box prompt injection achieved an 86.1% attack success rate across 36 real-world LLM-integrated services (reported from Liu et al. 2023b, not measured by this survey).

Janus / PII fine-tuning: fine-tuning GPT-3.5 on just 10 PII instances let it disclose 650 of 1000 target PIIs (vs. 0 without fine-tuning); the Janus few-shot method raised this to 699 (reported from Chen et al. 2023).
Human red-teaming: 2 highly effective prompts reached 99% attack success on GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 (reported from Shen et al. 2023a).
PAIR jailbreaks are reported to often succeed in under 20 queries against GPT-3.5/4 and Vicuna (reported from Chao et al. 2023).
Survey's own tally: Table 1 classifies 21 attack papers across 3 categories (Jailbreak, Prompt Injection, Data Poisoning) by threat model and evaluated LLMs.
baselines_compared: No experimental baselines -- the paper runs no experiments and compares no methods on a common metric.

As surveyed defense categories it contrasts external/moderation defenses (Baseline Defenses/Jain et al. 2023, SmoothLLM, Adversarial Prompt Shield (DistilBERT), Llama Guard, NeMo-Guardrails, Self-Examination) against internal/training defenses (Llama-2 supervised safety fine-tuning, RLHF safety reward modeling, Askell et al. context distillation).

Table 1 comparison axes: white/gray/black box threat model, prompt-vs-response and model-based attack surface, and list of evaluated LLMs (GPT-3.5/4, Vicuna, Claude, Llama-2, Falcon, Pythia, OPT, MiniGPT-4, BLIP-2, etc.).
